case_title: IN RE: MATERNAL AND CHILD HEALTH ACCESS

docket_entries.description: OPPOSITION TO MOTION FOR ISSUANCE OF PERREMPTORY WRIT OF MANDATE AND INJUNCTIVE RELIEF;DECLARATION OF SETH BRUNNER FILED BY RESPONDENT ALLENBY, CLIFF IN HIS OFFICIAL CAPACITY AS CHAIR OF THE MEDICAL RISK MANAGEMENT INSURANCE BOARD MANAGED RISK MEDICAL INSURANCE BOARD
MINI-MINUTES FOR NOV-25-2008 9:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, PETITIONER MATERNAL AND CHILD HEALTH ACCESS MOTION FOR ISSUANCE OF PEREMPTORY WRIT OF MANDATE AND INJUNCTIVE RELIEF (CCP SECTION 1085). RULING: NO APPEARANCE ON THIS MATTER. THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING - THE WRIT IS GRANTED. THE DECISION OF THE UNITED STATES SUPREME COURT IN MEMORIAL HOSPITAL V. MARICOPA COUNTY (1974) 415 U.S. 250, 262 MAKES CLEAR THAT A "DURATIONAL RESIDENCE REQUIREMENT FOR FREE MEDICAL CARE PENALIZES INDIGENTS FOR EXERCISING THEIR RIGHT TO MIGRATE TO AND SETTLE IN [CALIFORNIA]. ACCORDINGLY, THE CLASSIFICATION CREATED BY THE RESIDENCE REQUIREMENT, 'UNLESS SHOWN TO BE NECESSARY TO PROMOTE A COMPELLING STATE INTEREST, IS UNCONSTITUTIONAL.'" MEMORIAL HOSPITAL IS JUST ONE DECISION IN A LONG LINE OF SUPREME COURT DECISIONS STRIKING DOWN SUCH REQUIREMENTS. THE STATE HAS NOT OFFERED A REASON CONSISTENT WITH THE MANDATES OF THE CONSTITUTION TO JUSTIFY THE RESIDENT REQUIREMENT. ACCORDINGLY, THE WRIT IS GRANTED AND INSURANCE CODE SECTION 12698(A) IS DECLARED UNCONSTITUTIONAL. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER.) (302/PJM) JUDGE: PATRICK J. MAHONEY, REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR 8600

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion For Issuance Of Peremtory Writ Of Mandate And Injunctive Relief; Request For Judicial Notice
Off Calendar Dec-16-2008 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER MATERNAL AND CHILD HEALTH ACCESS Motion For Issuance Of Peremptory Writ Of Mandate And Injunctive Relief (CCP Section 1085).
RULING: No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling - The writ is granted. The decision of the United States Supreme Court in Memorial Hospital v. Maricopa County (1974) 415 U.S. 250, 262 makes clear that a "durational residence requirement for free medical care penalizes indigents for exercising their right to migrate to and settle in [California]. Accordingly, the classification created by the residence requirement, 'unless shown to be necessary to promote a compelling state interest, is unconstitutional.'" Memorial Hospital is just one decision in a long line of Supreme Court decisions striking down such requirements. The State has not offered a reason consistent with the mandates of the constitution to justify the resident requirement. Accordingly, the writ is granted and Insurance Code section 12698(a) is declared unconstitutional. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER.) (302/PJM/VC) Judge: Patrick J. Mahoney, Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600
